entry.title: Production Version automatic switch when BOM components inventory depleted
entry.content:
Hello

I am looking for some guidance on how to handle this situation.

I have a scenario where the FG current BOM has single component (CIB - Cabinet in a box) and we are transitioning to Many Components (between 5 to 10 items to build a cabinet). We need the planned order for MTS or Production Order for MTO to automatically select the alternate BOM when CIB inventory is depleted. 

I tried by setting material priority or group in the BOM but it is not working as I expected. The Planned Order always defaults to the main Item even though the inventory is 0 and material is flagged for discontinuation.

AI suggested to create 2 production structures 1 for CIB BOM and 2nd for many components for the FG. Then create a custom program to switch the version based on material availability. MRP 4 needs to be updated. Any suggestion how to solve the above by automation vs manually switching the Alt BOM in the Production Version.

Thank you

entry.title: Re: Delay items part of curricula to become available to...
entry.content:
Hi @Gi, 

That is not possible. Once you add an item into a curricula & assign it to user, you cannot delay it to be made available to user.

Instead you can try an alternative approach which deals with blended courses that has both class & online content. You can refer to the below KBA that breifs you the details about what exactly are the options & how the users would be accessing the course/content etc.

2552108 - Access to content of blended item from class 

Another suggestion is to have the items assigned through program, where in you can restrict users in a way that they must complete items sections wise & can only start next section if they had completed the previous section.

SAP Help Document - Learning Programs: Bundled Courses in a Syllabus

Apart from these, there are no other alternatives we can suggest.

entry.title: Re: Delay items part of curricula to become available to...
entry.content:
HI @YashwanthP  & @Gi ,

I agree and disagree with what you say Yash, as "Yes" it isn't possible to do this with a single Curricula & Assignment Profile.  But it is possible if you use a combination of three Curricula & associated Assignment Profiles.

Let's say that you have the end goal of Learners being competent in "Curricula ABC" (which contains the Learning Items A, B, & C) by the Assignment Profile that has the rules to include Learners that have the "Job Code" = "JC00159" and in the "Org (including sub Org's)" = "Corp (753951)", but as you say you want them to complete the Items in order without any of the later Items being seen on their Learning Plan (even if you have a staggered Required by / Initial Period).

If you first assign them "Curricula A" (which contains only the single Learning Item A) by the Assignment Profile A that has the rules to include Learners that have the "Job Code" = "JC00159" and in the "Org (including sub Org's)" = "Corp (753951)", but do not have Learning History for Item A.

Once they complete Item A, then they will not met the Assignment Profile A rules and it will remove "Curricula A", but this is when "Curricula AB" (which contains the Learning Items A & B) is assigned by the Assignment Profile AB that has the rules to include Learners that have the "Job Code" = "JC00159" and in the "Org (including sub Org's)" = "Corp (753951)", but do not have Learning History for Item B but does have learning history for Item A.

Once again when they complete Item B, then they will not met the Assignment Profile AB rules and it will remove "Curricula AB", but this is when "Curricula ABC" (which contains the Learning Items A, B & C) is assigned by the Assignment Profile ABC that has the rules to include Learners that have the "Job Code" = "JC00159" and in the "Org (including sub Org's)" = "Corp (753951)", and the Learning History for Item B and Item A.

I know that from an admin point of view that this is messy, but the end user/Learner gets the seemless experience of only ever seeing the single Item in their Learning Plan. 

I used a simplier version of this a lot with Inductions where the First/Initial training is a Instructor Led but the Refresher can be Online (which the Instructor Led is a substitute for) and it works brilliantly.

Note: In this example I often add the preceeding Item as a prerequisite because I've found that they will try and short cut their training if they can and just do the Online Refresher training

entry.title: Quality inspection at outbound with Extended warehouse management (EWM)
entry.content:
Inspection at outbound is demanded by many customers for specific products. Inspection on EWM managed outbound delivery was not possible till S/4HANA 2025 FPS0 release. Capability of inspection on outbound delivery with EWM has been introduced with S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 release. My present blog is to discuss on the functionality and the prerequisite configurations to achieve inspection with EWM managed outbound delivery.

A typical process flow for inspection for EWM managed outbound delivery is depicted in below image.

An outbound delivery order (ODO) 80004946 relevant for Q-Inspection at outbound at item level has been picked , packed but not yet goods issued.

An inspection lot 100000000050 has been created in S/4HANA for the material FG1015 for the outbound delivery 80004946 from sales order 10005725.

If the goods issue is triggered on the EWM ODO, then an error message appears aborting the goods issue as the usage decision on inspection lot is pending or the inspection lot has not been accepted.

Pre-requisite configuration and master data are

  • Inspection object type 8 (Q-Inspection Outbound) needs to be activated at SCM Extended Warehouse Management -> Extended Warehouse Management -> Cross-Process Settings -> Quality Management -> Basics and Integration -> Define and Activate Warehouse-Dependent IOTs.

  • Inspection lot origin needs to be assigned to delivery type

  • Inspection type needs to be assigned to delivery category

  • Quality info record needs to be created for the material with customer and sales org.

This functionality also works for

  • Embedded and decentral EWM (with backend S/4HANA 2025 FPS01)
  • Delivery type LO Delivery w/o Ref.
  • Material with batch management

QM with EWM outbound is a simple and useful solution closing a gap for feature parity of EWM with IM.

This blog is based on my personal tests , observations , insights in S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 release. Will appreciate your feedback / comments.

That way, the contract drives the billing relevance and ensures the six‑month free period is enforced automatically. If you want billing documents for reporting, you can allow the service order/confirmation to generate a zero‑value billing request.

entry.title: Re: Production Version automatic switch when BOM componen...
entry.content: If you want true stock‑sensitive automation, the best practice is to implement the production version determination BAdI. That way, the system dynamically chooses the correct BOM alternative based on inventory, without requiring manual switching or scheduled jobs.

entry.title: Re: Master Data Material Best Practice
entry.content:
You can explore the below approaches

1) Material Master Governance
Introduce a central master data team or workflow approval for new material creation. Users request new materials, but descriptions are standardized by the governance team.

2) Description Standardization Rules
Define a naming convention (e.g., “Product Type – Brand – Model – Specification”). Train users and enforce rules via documentation or validation checks.

3) Use Classification / Characteristics
Instead of relying only on free‑text descriptions, use material classification (e.g., brand, model, type as separate fields). This makes searching and reporting easier and reduces reliance on description text.

4) Duplicate Check Tools
Implement a duplicate check during material creation. Use SAP standard duplicate check (if available in your release). Or build a custom search routine that compares new descriptions against existing ones (ignoring word order, using keywords).

5) Leverage Product Hierarchy / Catalog
If spare parts or equipment are catalogued, link materials to a product hierarchy or catalog code. This ensures consistency and reduces free‑text variation.

entry.title: Re: Review Booklet - Create a Navigation Target
entry.content:
Good day @leonor_brites 

Thank you for your questions and screenshots.

The most likely cause is that the navigation target is passing a fixed text value instead of the actual document number field from your booklet row.

In your last screenshot, the target app opens and Order is filled with SALESORDER as literal text. That means the navigation itself is working, but the parameter value the system passed is wrong. So, your target receives exactly what you maintained.

See the following reference for more details

Creating Generic Report Application | SAP Help Portal

So, if you maintain parameter name as SalesDocument and value as SalesOrder, then the target app gets the SalesOrder text, not the row’s sales order number. This is exactly consistent with your screenshots, where the target app opens but shows SALESORDER in the document field instead of the real sales order ID.

Question: What could be causing this issue?

Answer: Most likely, you configured the navigation target with a static parameter value instead of a dynamic field mapping from the booklet data provider.

Question: Is there a configuration step that I might have missed?

Answer: Usually, the missing step is not on the app you are targeting itself. The missing step is in the navigation target mapping which you must ensure that:

  • The parameter is pointing to the actual source field that contains the sales order number
  • Not to a fixed text such as SalesOrder.

See the following reference for more details

Creating Generic Report Application | SAP Help Portal

I recommend you check you setup and confirm:

  • That the Semantic Object and Action keeps the one that correctly opens the target app
  • Your Field-level mapping contains the real sales order number
  • Your Application Parameter is only used if the target app truly requires a technical inbound parameter, and then bind it to the actual row value, not a fixed label.

Here is a quick guid for your system:

In your Navigation target, open Sales order navigation target

Then check the parameter row, if the value is something like SalesOrder, remove that fixed value.

At the field level, bind the navigation to the field in the booklet data source that contains the reals sales order number, run the booklet again and click the sales order link.

See the following reference for more insights

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a630d57fc5004c6383e7a81efee7a8bb/634525bea37b43d88a608e402221c932.html?locale=en-US

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Automatic Clearing of the pre-paid Withholding tax GL...
entry.content:
Good day @Komalb 

Thank you for your questions and screenshots.

What you want is understandable, but based on the standard Public Cloud behaviour, there is no documented standard configuration that automatically copies the invoice document reference into the payment document’s prepaid withholding tax line so that the two G/L items can be matched for automatic clearing. So, that means:

  • Automatic clearing is only possible if the invoice-side and payment-side open items share the same clearing criteria
  • If that common criterion is not populated on both documents, the system will not clear them automatically

For clarification, Clear Open Items-Automatic Clearing and related General Ledger Jobs is the equivalent app of classic F.13 In Public Cloud. See KBA 3147360 for more insights. So, the process to test is not classical on premise F.13 directly, but the Public Cloud automatic clearing framework.

The reason why your current idea does not work is because your proposed design depends on the is logic:

  1. Invoice document prepaid WHT line gets a reference or assignment,
  2. Payment document prepaid WHT clearing line gets the same reference or
  3. Assignment and automatic clearing matches both.

That would only work if the same matching field is truly present on both items. However, from your description:

  • The invoice side contains a usable reference
  • The payment side does not populate the same reference
  • Therefore, there is a lack of a common grouping criterion for automatic clearing to take place.

It is important to note that the key requirement remains that the same value must exist on both open times. See KBA 3147360 for more insights.

The recommended approach is to:

  1. Use automatic clearing only if you can establish a true common criterion. Which means, if both invoice and payment postings carry the same Assignment, Reference, or any other grouping field, then you can use Clear Open Items-Automatic Clearing app for the prepaid WHT G/L account. This would only work if both invoic and payment lines have the same value.
  2. Clear manually is the safe standard fallback. This means, if the payment document does not carry a shared criterion, then the safer and standard fallback is to use Clear G/L Accounts-Manual Clearing for this prepaid WHT account. This is most reliable if your system cannot generate a reliable shared key.

See the following reference for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/a84817567f98692de10000000a4450e5.html?locale=en-US

So, the best-practice interpretation conclusion for your Mexico scenario:

  • The standard withholding tax accounting flow is fine
  • Automatic clearing is not driven by tax logic alone
  • It still requires you having a shared clearing key
  • If no standard common field is populated on both postings, then manual G/L clearing is the correct process.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: How Invoice Scan Business Documents Transform Efficiency in SAP Enterprise Service Management (ESM)
entry.content:
Previous Article : https://community.sap.com/t5/crm-and-cx-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-enterprise-service-management-esm-how-to-enable-business-document/ba-p/14332556 

In today’s fast‑moving business environment, organizations are under pressure to process vendor invoices faster, more accurately, and with fewer manual steps. Traditional invoice processing—where teams manually enter data, verify suppliers, and route documents—slows down operations, introduces errors, and delays payments.

SAP Enterprise Service Management (ESM) changes the game with the Invoice Scan Business Document framework. This smart document‑processing capability enables companies to digitize invoices, automatically extract key information, and match it with the right customer or business partner from S/4 HANA with SAP ESM

Let’s explore how this works and why it matters.

What Is an Invoice Scan Business Document?

An Invoice Scan Business Document in SAP is a digital representation of an invoice created from a scanned file or an email attachment. Using SAP Document AI (DOCX AI) machine learning and OCR (Optical Character Recognition), SAP DOCX AI extracts structured data from unstructured content—such as:

  • Customer name
  • Address and contact data
  • Invoice number
  • Currency
  • PO number
  • Item details
  • Tax values
  • Payment Reference
  • Payment terms

This scanned document becomes a Business Document inside SAP ESM, which can be processed like any other system-native document.

How Invoice Scanning Speeds Up Processing

1. Automated Data Extraction

Instead of manually keying in vendor or customer details, SAP identifies key fields through:

  • SAP DOCX AI OCR text capture
  • Semantic understanding of invoice layouts
  • Machine learning models trained on global document formats

This eliminates the most time-consuming part of invoice processing: data entry.

2. Faster Customer Identification Using S/4HANA & ESM

One of the biggest challenges in invoice processing is matching the scanned invoice to the correct customer or service contract.

S/4HANA & ESM handles this automatically by comparing extracted information with master data records:

  • Customer name
  • Email domain
  • Contract or service order numbers
  • Address and tax identifiers
  • Contact details

If the system finds a unique match, it automatically assigns the business partner.
If not, it presents the most likely candidates for the processor to confirm.

This drastically reduces:

  • Lookup time
  • Assignment errors
  • Duplicate customer creation

3. Intelligent Error Detection

SAP Document AI automatically flags inconsistencies such as:

  • Missing PO number
  • Mismatched customer name
  • Incorrect totals
  • Expired or mismatched contracts

This allows finance or shared service teams to resolve issues early, not after posting.

4. Seamless Integration with Workflow

Once captured, the invoice can be routed for:

  • Approval
  • Verification
  • Posting
  • Payment

All using SAP Build Process Automation flexible workflow engine.

This enables:

  • End-to-end traceability
  • SLA-based approvals
  • Automatic posting when confidence levels are high

Why This Matters to Finance, Shared Services & ESM Teams

✔ Faster Invoice-to-Pay Cycle

Invoices move through the system more quickly, accelerating payment and improving vendor relationships.

✔ Reduced Manual Effort

Teams spend less time typing and more time analyzing.

✔ Higher Accuracy

Machine learning reduces human errors and improves data consistency.

✔ Better Compliance & Auditability

Every extracted value, system recommendation, and user decision is logged.

✔ Scalable Automation

Once trained, the system improves over time, reducing workload even further.

Example Scenario: Identifying the Customer Automatically

  1. A vendor emails a PDF invoice.
  2. SAP ESM creates a Case & Business Document automatically creates an Invoice Scan Business Document.
  3. SAP DOCX AI (OCR) extracts key data like customer ID, service order, or contract reference.
  4. S/4HANA & ESM compares this to its master data.
  5. The system:
    • Auto-assigns the correct customer when confidence is high, or
    • Suggests the top matches for confirmation.
  6. The invoice is routed for approval or automatically posted if rules allow.

Result:
An end-to-end automated workflow from incoming invoice → validated document → assigned customer → posting.

Conclusion: A Smarter Way to Handle Invoices

The combination of Invoice Scan Business Documents and SAP S/4HANA Public or Private Cloud & SAP ESM represents a major step forward in invoice automation. By intelligently extracting data, identifying the correct customer, and integrating with workflows, organizations can:

  • Cut processing time
  • Improve accuracy
  • Reduce administrative workload
  • Enhance compliance
  • Support strategic finance operations

As businesses continue their digital transformation journeys, smart document processing becomes not just an advantage—but a necessity.

entry.title: Re: New Pipeline Concept features with version 1.0.12: improved handling of Custom Header Properties
entry.content:
Hi @FrantisekV,

thanks for your proposal. I will add it to the list of potential improvement ideas so that we can discuss it internally and evaluate whether it could be included in future increments.

For similar requirements today, the recommended approach is to use the pre-processing exits available in XI and IDoc inbound flows. These were designed exactly for such scenarios where global or scenario-specific logic is required, for example logging, adding custom headers, or other manipulations at Step01. I understand that this approach may be less favorable since it requires calling another iFlow from the exit, but you can set the MPL log level to "Error" so that it does not appear in the MPL chain (normally it should be always successful).

More details about this pre-processing exits feature can be found here:
https://community.sap.com/t5/integration-blog-posts/new-feature-for-the-pipeline-for-cloud-integration-custom-preprocessing/ba-p/14199571

In general, we try to be careful and conscious when adding additional processing steps to the generic flows. These flows are often used in high-volume interfaces, and if a certain functionality is not used by a customer, even small overhead (for example, calling the Partner Directory or executing additional logic) could introduce unnecessary processing time. This is one of the reasons why we introduced the pre-processing exits, allowing customers to add custom logic only when needed.

Nevertheless, I will add your suggestion to our ideas list so we can discuss whether it should become part of the generic flows or remain an option implemented via a customer-specific pre-processing exit.

Thanks again for the proposal, and please don’t hesitate to continue sharing ideas. Feedback like this helps us identify what could be improved and what might currently be missing.

Best regards,
Slava

entry.title: Re: Overview Page in SAP Business Application Studio using SAP's ES5 Service
entry.content:
@Asif_Ali1 ,

Please follow the below path to add any card in the SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) tool.

Path:

Explorer
→ Right Click on overview_page Project
→ Select Open Guided Development
→ Choose Overview Page
→ Select the required option:

  • Add a Custom Card to an Overview Page

  • Add a List Card to an Overview Page

  • Add a Table Card to an Overview Page

  • Add a Link List Card to an Overview Page

  • Add an Analytical Card to an Overview Page

Using this path, you can easily add any required card to the Overview Page in BAS.

 

You can choose the appropriate card as per the client requirement.

entry.title: Re: Generic IDoc Outbound flow in Cloud Integration Pipeline
entry.content:
Hi @FrantisekV ,
sorry, I just realized that I had not replied to your message earlier.
I actually share your concerns here. Even though the implementation with global variables is quite smart, it is still more of a workaround to bypass a product limitation and unfortunately it is not very scalable in the long term.

There is an Influence request exactly for this topic which you might want to support: https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/idea/365217 
However, it is currently not clear whether improvements will come to the existing IDoc/SOAP adapters or whether more advanced EOIO handling will be introduced mainly through the future IDoc/RFC adapter in EIC.

Regarding skipping sequence numbers / failed IDocs - unfortunately, I am not aware of any automatic mechanism that would skip a sequence number. On the SAP backend side, the typical approach is the manual handling via BD102_EOIO (Outbound Registry) / BD103_EOIO (Inbound Registry), as you mentioned. I am not aware of exits that would allow this to be automated in a clean way.

Best regards,
Slava

entry.title: DEIP — Domain Enterprise Integration Pattern: A Semantic Control Plane for Enterprise Integration
entry.content:
Disclaimer

Between March 12 and 13, 2026, during a late-night architecture review, the model I had been developing finally crystallized.
What had initially started as two SAP-oriented patterns converged into a five-layer semantic integration architecture, culminating in

DEIP – Domain Enterprise Integration Pattern.

On February 7, 2026 the scope of this work was limited to two patterns designed for SAP landscapes:

  • DCRP – Domain-Centric Routing Pattern
  • PDCP – Package Domain-Centric Pattern

Both focused on solving structural governance problems in SAP API Management and SAP Cloud Integration.

In just 36 days this initial nucleus evolved into a fully formalized five-layer semantic control architecture, designed to address a problem that many integration architects recognize but rarely formalize: integration sprawl.

This problem is not new.

For 17 years I have observed the same structural pattern repeating itself across different generations of SAP integration technologies:

  • SAP XI – fragmented Software Components
  • SAP PI/PO – interface and configuration proliferation
  • SAP BTP Integration Suite – proxy, package, credential and artifact sprawl inside CPI landscapes
  • SAP API Management - Proxy Sprawl Apocalypse
  • EDA and SOLACE in the same landscape for event mesh, what ? Serio ?
  • Others.....

Each generation improved the technology stack, but the structural indexing problem remained the same: integrations were organized by system or project, rather than by domain intent.

During these 36 days of research and experimentation, this recurring chaos was distilled into a single architectural model.

At the center of this model sits DEIP, the parent architecture layer responsible for governing the three execution patterns:

DEIP — Semantic Control Plane Decision Model
└── Decision Pipeline
       → Intent
       → Interaction Type
       → Quality Profile
       → Integration Capability
       → Protocol Profile
       → Platform Binding
    └── SDIA — Semantic Domain Integration Architecture
           (DEIP Reference Architecture)
        ├── GDCR — Gateway Domain-Centric Routing
        │        Reference Implementation — recommended starting point
        ├── DDCR — Domain Driven-Centric Router
        │        Routing and capability resolution engine
        └── ODCP — Orchestration Domain Composition Pattern
                 Cross-domain orchestration layer
     └── Execution Platforms

    SAP BTP · AWS · Azure · Kong · Kafka · IoT · AI/LLM · etc.

Together, these patterns form a domain-centric semantic control plane that sits above any specific execution platform.

From this point of closure — looking at SAP landscapes where the required capabilities already exist but are often applied without consistent domain semantics — this final blog post of the series begins.

Disclaimer SAP

SAP BTP Integration Suite already provides all the capabilities necessary to implement and validate this architecture in practice.

The platform offers:

  • a mature API Management layer
  • a powerful Cloud Integration runtime
  • Event Mesh
  • security services
  • and strong governance tooling.

The problem has never been the SAP platform itself.

Instead, the issue often lies in how integrations are indexed and governed.Many landscapes organize integrations by projects and systems, rather than by domain intent, which leads to proxy, package and credential sprawl even on top of a strong platform.

This same concern was raised earlier in January 2026 in my SAP Community article:

 Clean Architecture vs The Frankenstein Landscape

This blog originated from a situation of chaos that I observed in several customer landscapes.

The SAP BTP Integration Suite was available, but the way integration responsibilities were distributed made the architecture unnecessarily fragmented.

In some environments the pattern looked like this:

  • SAP API Management used only for standard APIs

  • SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) used only for standard packages

  • MuleSoft API-Led used for custom APIs

  • Boomi acting as a cloud middleware for custom integrations

And that was only part of the picture.

In many landscapes additional platforms were also introduced for other integration styles:

  • Event Mesh / Event Brokers

  • Kafka

  • IBM MQ

  • IoT platforms

  • Queue-based middleware

  • Streaming platforms

Each new integration requirement often resulted in another platform, another gateway, or another integration layer.

When I saw these architectures repeating themselves across different customers, a simple question came to my mind:

Is this really serious architecture?

Does this actually make sense from an integration perspective ? 

A kind of Frankenstein landscape built from overlapping platforms and responsibilities?

For me, the answer was no.

In most cases this situation is not driven by real technical constraints.
It is usually the result of organizational fragmentation, vendor tendency, or what I call “architects by manual” — architects who simply follow platform playbooks instead of questioning the architecture itself.

In many cases the result looks like multiple platforms overlapping while performing essentially the same functions.

The landscape becomes fragmented across different technologies and integration styles: APIs, events, queues, messaging platforms and middleware layers. What should be a coherent integration architecture slowly turns into a patchwork of tools and responsibilities.

This creates several structural problems.

There is multi-protocol exposure without clear consolidation, where the same business capability appears through different protocols such as REST, SOAP, OData, BAPI, RFC or IDoc, often managed by separate proxies and platforms.

At the same time, there is no centralized governance for integration decisions. Each team chooses tools, protocols or patterns independently, which leads to architectural inconsistency.

The consequences are predictable:

  • overlapping platforms performing the same functions

  • lack of centralized governance

  • high maintenance cost

  • complex operational landscapes

  • fragmented teams with different platform specializations

  • limited collaboration around the business domain itself

Instead of organizing integration around the domain, the architecture becomes organized around tools and vendors.

From my perspective, this is where the real problem begins.

When I look at these landscapes today, after studying integration structures more deeply, I often see something that resembles an architectural collapse in slow motion. Too many tools, too many integration surfaces, and too little structural coherence.

That realization pushed me to start researching this problem more seriously.

I kept studying, experimenting, and trying to understand how integration architecture could be organized from a more fundamental perspective.

One of the questions that kept coming back was simple:

Do we really need six different proxies for the same capability?

If the same business capability is exposed through multiple protocols – for example REST, SOAP and OData – does it really need to result in separate integration surfaces every time? Or can we centralize these capabilities behind a smaller number of domain‑driven HTTPS entry points, instead of multiplying proxies and integration layers across platforms ? 

And if we look one level deeper, the same question applies to the underlying protocols themselves. Why do we need so many protocols for the same domain behaviour? In many cases, BAPIs, RFCs and IDocs can be exposed as HTTPS web services (REST, SOAP or OData) behind a single semantic façade. Centralizing domain capabilities over HTTPS does not remove backend diversity, but it drastically reduces the number of exposed surfaces and makes the integration landscape governable again.

The same question applies to SAP Cloud Integration.

Do we really need dozens of CPI packages and technical users for the same domain?

If one business domain is implemented across multiple backends – SAP S/4HANA, Salesforce, legacy ERPs, logistics providers – does it really require a separate CPI package and a new technical user for every single interface and project?

Or can we consolidate these flows into a small number of domain‑centric packages, with tiered technical users aligned to sub‑domains, instead of multiplying packages and credentials until the landscape becomes ungovernable?

These questions eventually led me to formalize the architecture that I now call:

DEIP — Domain Enterprise Integration Pattern.

DEIP was designed precisely to address this type of architectural fragmentation by starting from the domain, rather than from the technology or the platform.

From there, the integration architecture can be derived in a deterministic way.

My research is not perfect and it is still evolving, but the goal is simple: to provide a starting point for bringing structural coherence back into integration architectures.

Wherever I work, that is the direction I intend to follow — organizing integration around the domain, not around fragmented platforms.

As a way of acknowledging the SAP ecosystem that shaped much of this work over the past 17 years, I am making a small professional commitment.

Based on my experience working with several API management platforms – including five different API gateways during the research – SAP API Management stands out for its user interface, configuration simplicity and proxy creation experience.

However, an important realization emerged during this research: when a  DDCR (Domain Driver Centric Router) is introduced, the role of the API gateway itself changes significantly.

At that point, the gateway is no longer just a platform for exposing and managing URLs.

It becomes part of a metadata‑driven control plane, where the final destination is resolved deterministic dynamically from domain intent, metadata and lightweight routing logic.In practical terms, this means that any API management platform – regardless of vendor – can operate at the same architectural level when combined with DDCR.

The gateway stops being merely a URL manager and becomes a decoupling layer between intent and destination, resolved through metadata and deterministic routing.


In this model, SAP API Management evolves from a traditional API gateway into a domain‑driven integration entry point, where routing decisions are driven by the control plane rather than statically defined inside individual proxies.

Alignment with SAP Strategic Directions

This work aligns directly with three strategic directions already promoted by SAP:

  • Clean Core
    • DEIP keeps protocol complexity and integration decision logic outside of S/4HANA by moving these concerns into a semantic control plane on SAP BTP.
    • This allows the core ERP to remain standard while integrations stay governed and evolvable.
  • RISE with SAP
    • By indexing integrations on domain intent rather than on individual systems, DEIP provides RISE transformation programs with a structured way to rationalize legacy interfaces during cloud migrations.
  • ISA-M
    • DEIP complements ISA-M by operating as a decision layer above the integration scenario model.While ISA-M classifies integration scenarios, DEIP maps them to capabilities, protocol profiles and platform bindings, ensuring that integration architectures remain domain-driven and consistent across platforms.

the intent of the domain.

In this model, domain intent becomes the organizing principle that allows architectural decisions — from interaction patterns to protocol profiles and platform bindings — to be navigated consistently across the enterprise integration landscape.

Ricardo Luz Holanda Viana - March 2026

Introduction

Enterprise integration landscapes have evolved significantly over the past two decades.
Technologies have improved, platforms have matured, and new capabilities have been added to integration stacks across the industry.

Yet one structural problem persists across almost every enterprise landscape: integration sprawl.

Interfaces are frequently indexed by systems, projects, or technical artifacts, rather than by the intent of the domain they serve. Over time this leads to landscapes filled with proxies, packages, credentials, endpoints and flows that are technically functional, but structurally difficult to understand, govern and evolve.

This blog introduces DEIP — Domain Enterprise Integration Pattern, a vendor-agnostic semantic meta-framework structured across five architectural levels, governed entirely as a metadata control plane, designed to bring structural clarity to modern enterprise integration landscapes.

At its core, DEIP proposes a simple but powerful shift:
integration decisions should begin with domain intent, not with technology.

From that starting point, integration architectures can be navigated through a structured decision pipeline that determines:

  • the interaction type

  • the required quality characteristics

  • the integration capability

  • the protocol profile

  • and finally the platform binding.

This model forms a semantic control plane that governs runtime architectures such as SDIA, GDCR, DDCR and ODCP, while remaining independent from any specific execution platform.

The goal of this article is not to dive into every technical detail of the model.
Instead, it aims to provide architects with a clear conceptual overview of the architecture and the problem it addresses.

A complete technical companion document — expected in the coming months — will describe in detail how to map domains, structure the decision model, and navigate the architectural layers in real enterprise landscapes.

Until then, the most important idea to retain is simple:

everything begins with a single primary key: the intent of the domain. - "The Domains never lies"


Interpretation Note

This timeline should not be interpreted as positioning DEIP as “higher or better” to the foundational work of Hohpe & Woolf (2003).

Enterprise Integration Patterns defined 65 fundamental patterns describing how messages move through enterprise integration systems. Their work remains one of the most important references in the field and continues to guide integration architecture today.

The name Domain Enterprise Integration Pattern (DEIP) was deliberately chosen as a tribute to that work. Hohpe and Woolf defined the patterns that govern how messages travel inside enterprise integration systems.

DEIP builds on that legacy by addressing a different architectural question that emerged as enterprise landscapes evolved into multi-cloud, multi-vendor environments and AI-driven systems.

While Enterprise Integration Patterns describe how messages move across integration infrastructure, DEIP proposes a structural model for how domain intent moves across the enterprise landscape.

In this model, the domain becomes the primary architectural key, allowing integration decisions to be navigated consistently across interaction types, capabilities, protocol profiles and platform bindings.

In that sense, DEIP should be understood as an evolutionary architectural layer built on top of the foundations established by Enterprise Integration Patterns, extending their ideas into modern enterprise integration landscapes.

This work is therefore presented not as a replacement of those foundations, but as a continuation of them — an attempt to take the principles established in 2003 and structure them for the next generation of enterprise integration architectures.

What is DEIP?

DEIP – the Domain Enterprise Integration Pattern – is a semantic decision architecture designed to bring structural clarity to modern enterprise integration landscapes. Instead of organizing integrations by systems, projects or technical artifacts, DEIP proposes that every integration decision starts from a different primary key: the intent of the domain. In this model, the domain becomes the organizing principle used to navigate integration decisions across the entire landscape, and a semantic control plane replaces ad‑hoc design inside individual flows, proxies or API configurations.

The DEIP Decision Pipeline (6 stages)

At the center of DEIP is a six‑stage decision pipeline:

Intent → Interaction Type → Quality Profile → Integration Capability → Protocol Profile → Platform Binding  

Each stage answers a different question and progressively transforms domain intent into a concrete integration implementation:

  • Intent – What is the semantic purpose of the integration?
    Examples: invoice.read.finance, orders.create.ecommerce, iot.sensor.stream, security.identity.authenticate.

  • Interaction Type – Which interaction family does it belong to?
    Examples: request, command, event, stream, batch, workflow, replication.

  • Quality Profile – What non‑functional characteristics are required?
    Examples: low latency, high throughput, at‑least‑once delivery, strict ordering, strong authentication, data sovereignty.

  • Integration Capability – Which abstract integration capability is needed?
    Examples: API mediation, event streaming, CDC replication, long‑running workflow, IoT ingestion, AI tool routing, security authentication/authorization.

  • Protocol Profile – Which concrete protocol or messaging style implements that capability?
    Examples: HTTP/REST, gRPC, OData, Kafka, MQTT, AMQP, ETL/CDC, OpenID Connect.

  • Platform Binding – On which runtime platform will this flow execute?
    Examples: SAP API Management, SAP Cloud Integration, SAP Event Mesh, Kafka cluster, IoT broker, identity provider, Kubernetes/Istio.

By navigating these seven stages, architects can consistently determine how integrations should be implemented, independent of specific vendors or products.

Metadata as the Control Mechanism

DEIP does not introduce another integration product. It operates purely as a semantic control plane expressed as metadata that describes the integration landscape. This metadata acts as the control map of the architecture and can live in a structured database, a metadata catalog, a governance repository, or—even in the early stages—a well‑structured spreadsheet.

Metadata control plane = a table where you store your enterprise integration decisions.

Can be:
- Excel          ← it's metadata
- Google Sheets  ← it's metadata
- Database       ← it's metadata
- Redis / KVM    ← it's metadata
- Sticky note on the wall ← technically also metadata

The fancy name is just that — a table where you store the map of intentions.

The sophistication is not WHERE you store it. It's WHAT you store and how DDCR uses it.

A simple starting point can look like this:

Domain                 Intent                     Interaction         Capability                     Protocol           Platform

OrdersCreate OrderAsync, eventEvent publicationKafkaAWS
CustomersUpdate ProfileSync APIRequest/ResponseRESTSAP BTP
PaymentsPayment ConfirmationEvent (notification)Event notificationAMQPAzure
IoTSensor Telemetry IngestionStreamTelemetry stream ingestionMQTT → KafkaIoT Hub + Kafka/K8s
IoTDevice Firmware UpdateCommandReliable device commandMQTT QoS 1IoT Hub
ServiceProvision Application SpaceAsync workflowProvisioning orchestrationREST + WebhookSAP BTP
ServiceRestart Critical ServiceCommandOperational runbookgRPC/RESTKubernetes / Istio
SecurityUser AuthenticationRequestAuthentication serviceOpenID ConnectIdentity Provider
SecurityThreat Detection AlertEvent (alert)Security event routingHTTPS / JSONSIEM
SecurityCredential RotationBatch / workflowSecret lifecycle managementAPI / CLIVault / KMS
 
Over time, this table evolves into a living architectural map of the enterprise integration landscape, where each row is a governed decision path through the DEIP pipeline.

From Decision Model to Runtime Architecture

DEIP itself is not a runtime system. It is an architectural decision model that governs runtime architectures such as:

  • SDIA – Semantic Domain Integration Architecture

  • GDCR – Gateway Domain‑Centric Routing

  • DDCR – Domain‑Driven Centric Router

  • ODCP – Orchestration Domain Composition Pattern

These patterns implement the decisions derived from the DEIP pipeline inside real integration platforms (for example, SAP API Management, SAP Cloud Integration, Event Mesh, IoT brokers, service meshes and identity providers). The semantic control plane decides what should happen and where; the runtime patterns execute those decisions.

The core principle behind all of this remains intentionally simple and universal:
every integration decision starts from a single primary key — the intent of the domain.

DEIP and DDCR — A Simple Mathematical Model

Although DEIP is primarily an architectural decision model, its pipeline can also be expressed as a simple mathematical function that transforms domain intent into a concrete integration implementation.

Friends from SAP, I’m going to present the mathematical formulas that I derived. But don’t worry — the solution is DEIP itself. The formulas are only here as a visual aid to help understand the model.

What matters is the architectural, structural and execution vision behind it. At this point, the way I see integration architecture is very simple: I see patterns, domains and mathematics.

Mathematics is universal - 2 + 2 = 4 anywhere in the world.

The DEIP pipeline is a sequence of deterministic functions. Each stage is a formal reduction where context constrains the valid outputs:

f₁ : I × C → T
Intent + Context → Interaction Type

f₂ : I × T × C → Q
Intent + Interaction Type + Context → Quality Profile

f₃ : I × T × Q → Cap
Intent + Interaction Type + Quality Profile → Integration Capability

f₄ : Cap → Pprot
Integration Capability → Protocol Profile

f₅ : Pprot × C → Pplat
Protocol Profile + Context → Platform Binding

In this model:

I     = Domain Intent
C     = Context (environment, constraints, policies)
T     = Interaction Type
Q     = Quality Profile
Cap   = Integration Capability
Pprot = Protocol Profile
Pplat = Platform Binding

 

DDCR Resolution Model - Remember in SAP APIM it's just the javascript policy the name is my creation just it.

Once the decision pipeline produces a platform binding, the DDCR engine resolves the final runtime configuration using deterministic key resolution.

{ f(key) → value }

Where the key represents the resolved integration identity derived from the DEIP pipeline, and the value represents the runtime configuration to be applied (routing target, protocol adapter, credentials, endpoint, or orchestration behavior).

Look at the table below for example. The Order to Cash process is essentially the same anywhere in the world.

So how does DEIP interpret this?

No matter where you are in the world, regardless of language or platform, the entry point for any Order to Cash call is:

/<domain>/<entity>/<action>/<target>

Example:

/sales/order/create

What does this mean inside SAP API Management ?

Only one proxy is required. It can be defined using a single OpenAPI or Swagger specification with an entry point like:

/sales/orders/create/<target>

The routing is static, and a JavaScript policy (DDCR) resolves the final destination.

This means you can place 20 vendors behind a single API proxy in SAP API Management.

The code can include custom headers for tracking, and additional headers can also be added if needed.

This approach eliminates proxy sprawl at the root.

That is the idea behind DDCR.

In practice, the DDCR runtime proved this approach works. A simple JavaScript policy implementation processed around 2,067,904 requests across 8 platforms and 5 programming languages, running continuously for about 29 hours, withzero routing failures — 158 network-layer failures only (ECONNRESET/ETIMEDOUT)

The full implementation details, experiments and runtime evidence are available in the GitHub repository associated with this work.

 

DEIP(Intent, Context) → Implementation

 

Applying DEIP in Practice

Once the DEIP decision pipeline is defined, the next step is applying its outcomes inside the runtime architecture.

DEIP itself does not execute integrations.
Instead, it acts as a semantic control plane that guides how runtime components should behave.

In practice, the pipeline outputs are implemented through the SDIA architecture and its execution patterns.

DEIP → SDIA

The first layer where DEIP becomes operational is SDIA (Semantic Domain Integration Architecture).

SDIA represents the runtime architecture organized by domain, where integrations are grouped and governed according to their domain intent.

Instead of organizing integration artifacts by systems or projects, SDIA structures them by domains, such as:

Orders
Payments
Customers
Logistics
Notifications

Each domain becomes a semantic boundary where integration decisions derived from the DEIP pipeline are applied consistently.

SDIA → GDCR

At the gateway layer, DEIP decisions guide GDCR (Gateway Domain-Centric Routing).

GDCR is responsible for routing requests according to the domain intent.

Instead of routing based only on technical endpoints, GDCR uses the domain structure to determine where requests should flow.

Example:

/<domain>/<entity>/<action>/<target | sender>

   /sales/orders/create/salesforce
   /hr/customers/update/workday
   /finance/payments/authorize/stripe
   /iot/sensors/stream/mqtt
   /iot/devices/command/firmware

Here the domain is the primary routing key.

 Proxy Sprawl (1 proxy por backend or Contract)
   /sap-s4hana/orders/create
   /salesforce-crm/orders/create
   /oracle-erp/orders/create
   /legacy-system/orders/create
↑ 4 proxies · 4 contracts · 4 versioning

After with DEIP + SDIA + GDCR and DDCR

GDCR (1 proxy por domínio)

    /sales/<entity>/<action>/<vendor>

↑ 1 proxy · 1 contract · new vendor via metadata - KVM for SAP APIM

 

Implementation Note – PoC vs. Productive Landscapes
In all documents the /sales/<entity>/<action>/<target> pattern is used as a minimal PoC example to prove the DDCR invariant and the DEIP decision pipeline. This URL structure is valid for experimental validation, where a single GDCR proxy for /sales/ receives all Sales traffic. However, in a productive enterprise landscape this path should be extended with an explicit business subdomain to avoid over‑concentration of routes and to keep semantic separation between processes. A more robust pattern is:

/\<domain\>/\<subdomain\>/\<entity\>/\<action\>/\<target | sender\>

For example, within the SALES domain:

  • /sales/o2c/orders/create/salesforce – Order‑to‑Cash

  • /sales/l2o/leads/create/hubspot – Lead‑to‑Opportunity

  • /sales/q2o/quotes/approve/cpq – Quote‑to‑Order

In this model, you typically create one GDCR proxy per domain and subdomain, such as gdcr-proxy-domain-sales-o2c, with static routing on the /sales/o2c/<entity>/... segment, while DDCR remains responsible for resolving the final target via metadata. PoC examples using only /sales/ should therefore be interpreted as experimental simplifications, not as a recommended productive topology.

Entry door: https://<apim>/282cc9datrial/sales/ 

What does this configuration mean?

This is a static routing rule condition in the API Proxy design. This single proxy — SALES O2C — supports all calls from anywhere, covering 13 business processes from the O2C domain.

What does this still mean ?

500 applications · 1 proxy · 13 routing rules = End of proxy sprawl. The rest DDCR — JavaScript — resolves alone. Don't worry.

Why did I decide to segregate the proxy per business domain? To avoid the sprawl of a single proxy responsible for all calls. This segregation also keeps everything more organized.

Does DDCR work with OpenAPI and Swagger ?

Yes — the JavaScript is the routing engine. There is no connection with the contract or SLA of the specification. But the mindset must change: you don't create a specification per vendor — you create a specification per domain.

This means the specification, proxy, and DDCR JavaScript are immutable. If anything new comes to onboard — add one string in the KVM. That's it. As simple as 2+2=4. This is mathematics.

But my company is working with IOT etc... so follow the same process, create proxy, add the routing, what will be the domain /LLM/ thats it

gdcr-ai-llm-proxy-domain-unified

    https://<apim>/282cc9datrial/llm

SDIA → DDCR

Once a request enters the domain structure, DDCR (Domain Driven Centric Router) resolves the final integration configuration.

DDCR acts as a deterministic resolution engine.

It takes the resolved domain identity and looks up the metadata produced by the DEIP pipeline to determine:

  • protocol - http

  • endpoint - any destiny

Conceptually:

Domain + Intent → Configuration

So let's check again what was discussed above we are speaking about SALES - O2C means the entry door for SAP APIM:

Entry door: https://<apim>/282cc9datrial/sales/ 

Than we have this cenario - 1 proxy - routing ( /orders/** - / customers/** - /deliveries/** 😞

https://<sapapim>/sales/orders/create/salesforce
https://<sapapim>/sales/orders/update/salesforceemea
https://<sapapim>/sales/orders/create/microsoft
https://<sapapim>/sales/customers/sync/s4hana
https://<sapapim>/sales/customers/sync/shopify
https://<sapapim>/sales/deliveries/transfer/fedex

DDCR Keys — KVM (determinísticas e machine-computed)

dcrporderscid01salesforce:http
dcrporderscid02sfemea:http
dcrporderscid03microsoft:http
dcrpcustomerssid04s4hana:cxf
dcrpcustomerssid05shopify:http
dcrpdeliveriestid06fedex:http

Resolved URLs — CPI (execution-ready)

https://<cpi>/http/dcrp/orders/c/id01
https://<cpi>/http/dcrp/orders/u/id02
https://<cpi>/http/dcrp/orders/c/id03
https://<cpi>/cxf/dcrp/customers/s/id04
https://<cpi>/http/dcrp/customers/s/id05
https://<cpi>/http/dcrp/deliveries/t/id06

Again — this logic is pure mathematics. The only way to fail is a wrong intent HTTP path or a wrong string in the KVM, that triggers in the 3rd step the FAST-FAIL M2M in ~0.1ms, the backend don't even know about this call. It never fails. Over 2 million calls across 5 different API platforms from the market — and everything else I tested — zero routing failures.

SDIA → ODCP

Some integrations require orchestration across multiple domains. This is where ODCP — Orchestration Domain-Centric Pattern — operates.

ODCP governs three things simultaneously:

Package Topology — One Domain, One Package

Before:                          After:
Salesforce_Orders_Pkg        →   nx.sales.o2c.integrations
SAP_S4HANA_Orders_Pkg        →   (same package — Sales domain)
SAP_ECC_FI_AR_v2_Package     →   nx.finance.r2r.integrations
FedEx_Shipping_Integration   →   nx.logistics.le.integrations
Ariba_Procurement_Pkg        →   nx.procurement.s2p.integrations
Temp_QA_DoNotDelete          →   eliminated

39 packages → 4. Package count becomes invariant — it scales with domains, not vendors.

ODCP defines a structured package naming convention that encodes domain identity, business process, and platform context into a single readable string:

Component

Description

Example Values

Constraints

prefix

Organization or platform identifier

nx, sap, az, aws, mule

Lowercase, alphanumeric

domain

Business domain

sales, finance, logistics, procurement, hr, manufacturing

Lowercase, no spaces

process

Primary business process subdomain

o2c, r2r, le, s2p, h2r, p2p

Standard process codes

integrations

Fixed suffix

integrations

Always literal 'integrations'

[prefix].[domain].[process].integrations  Multi-division — same domain, same business process, one division owns the integration:

[prefix-company].[division].[domain].[businessprocess].[integration]

Multi-division — same orchestration tenant, multiple divisions, same domain:

[prefix-company].[division].[core].[domain]

iFlow DNA — Deterministic Artifact Naming

POST /sales/orders/create/salesforce
→ id01.o2c.salesforce.order.s4hana.c.in.sync

POST /finance/invoices/create/s4hana
→ id13.r2r.s4hana.invoice.target.c.in.sync

Every artifact is traceable from semantic URL → DDCR key → iFlow DNA. No more Salesforce_Order_Sync_v3_FINAL_USE_THIS.

id[seq].[subdomain].[sender].[entity].[receiver].[action].[direction].[sync|async]

Example:  id01.o2c.salesforce.order.s4hana.c.in.sync

Business intent enters at the semantic address. Metadata decides at the DDCR engine. Domain governs at the ODCP orchestration layer. Technology becomes background. Semantics become the integration language — end to end.

Credential Segmentation — Sub-Domain Tiered

cr.sales.orders.rw      — Orders only · 30 days rotation
cr.sales.analytics.ro   — Analytics only · 90 days rotation
cr.finance.ap.rw        — Accounts Payable only · 30 days rotation

39 credentials → 12. One rotation failure affects only one sub-domain — never the entire landscape.

The result: Business intent enters at the semantic URL. DDCR resolves. ODCP governs the artifact. Technology becomes background.

Credential Naming Convention

Technical user names follow the same domain‑centric semantic pattern as packages and flows:

Examples:

  • cr.sales.orders.rw – Sales / Orders / Read‑Write
  • cr.finance.ap.rw – Finance / Accounts Payable / Read‑Write
  • cr.finance.reporting.ro – Finance / Reporting / Read‑Only
  • cr.logistics.shipments.rw – Logistics / Shipments / Read‑Write

The naming convention makes credential ownership, scope, and permission level immediately readable from the identifier, eliminating the opacity of names like iflow_cred_12 or api_user_SF_orders.

Three Pillars of ODCP Credential Governance

 

Pillar

Principle

Outcome

Consolidation

Group flows by domain sub-domain, not by project or vendor

Eliminates credential silos and naming chaos

Standardization

Enforce consistent naming: prefix.domain.subdomain.permission

Enables systematic credential audit and rotation

Governance

Assign ownership by sub-domain architect; enforce RBAC on credential mutation

Centralized control, isolated blast radius, audit-ready

 

SAP-Specific Artifact Scope

In SAP BTP Cloud Integration, the iFlow DNA naming convention extends to all integration artifacts within the package — not only iFlows. Script collections, value mappings, message mappings, data stores, communication channels, technical users, and even internal variables adopt the same domain‑centric prefixing (for example ,vm.sales.order.status, sc.sales.order.transformation, cr.sales.orders.rw). This makes the entire CPI artifact space navigable by business domain rather than by technical type or vendor.

Artifact Type

Naming Convention

Integration Flows (iFlows)

id[seq].[subdomain].[sender].[entity].[receiver].[action].[direction].[mode]

Value Mapping Tables

vm.[domain].[entity].[field] — e.g. vm.sales.order.status

Script Collections

sc.[domain].[function] — e.g. sc.sales.order.transformation

Message Mappings

mm.[domain].[entity].[sender].[receiver] — e.g. mm.sales.order.sfdc.s4h

Technical Users

cr.[domain].[subdomain].[permission] — e.g. cr.sales.orders.rw

Internal Variables

var.[domain].[context].[name] — e.g. var.sales.orders.batchsize

 

SDIA — COMPLETE ARCHITECTURE

End-to-End Semantic Flow

The Semantic Domain Integration Architecture (SDIA) preserves business domain semantics across every layer of the enterprise integration stack. ODCP is the final governance layer in this chain — ensuring that semantic continuity extends from the API consumer address to the backend execution artifact.

Human Semantic Address

POST /sales/orders/create/salesforce

GDCR

Gateway Layer

Semantic gateway facade  —  domain-centric API governance

Single proxy per domain  ·  semantic URL abstraction  ·  fail-fast enforcement

DDCR

Resolution Engine

Deterministic 7-stage runtime resolution  —  metadata-driven backend binding

dcrporderscsalesforceid01:http  →  /http/dcrp/orders/c/salesforce/id01

ODCP

Orchestration Layer

Domain-centric package topology  —  iFlow DNA  —  tiered credential governance

nx.sales.o2c.integrations  ·  id01.o2c.salesforce.order.s4hana.c.in.sync

Enterprise Systems

SAP S/4HANA  ·  Salesforce  ·  Oracle  ·  Workday  ·  Stripe  ·  Legacy Systems  ·  Cloud Platforms

The Result

When combined, these layers create a complete architecture:

DEIP    — Semantic Control Plane      → What · Why · Which
   ↓
SDIA    — Domain Runtime Architecture → Organized by domain intent
   ↓
GDCR    — Gateway Layer               → 1 proxy per domain · immutable
   ↓
DDCR    — Resolution Engine           → f(key) → value · zero failures
   ↓
ODCP    — Orchestration Layer         → 1 package · DNA naming · tiered credentials
   ↓
Execution Platforms                   → SAP · AWS · Azure · Kong · IoT · AI/LLM
   ↓
Result  = Sales + Sales + Sales + Sales

The full end-to-end — based on the intention of the Sales domain. The technology does not matter. What matters is what the domain needs.

Conclusion

Most enterprise integration landscapes converge to a small set of recurring failure modes. Not because of bad engineers. Not because of bad platforms. Because the organizing principle was always wrong.

Technology was never the primary key. The domain always was.

DEIP does not add a new tool to your stack. It changes the question you ask before you build anything:

What is the domain intent of this integration?

Answer that first. Everything else — protocol, platform, proxy, credential, artifact — becomes a consequence.

The domain never lies. It never has. We just forgot to ask it first.

Start Here — Reading Order

⚠️Before reading anything else — start with GDCR.

GDCR is where everything began. Originally developed as DCRP and PDCP — two SAP-specific patterns — it evolved into the foundation of the entire SDIA ecosystem. Without reading GDCR first, the other documents will not make sense. For full understanding, the recommended starting point is GDCR.

Reading order:

  1. 🔗GDCR — DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18582492 ← Start here
  2. 🔗DDCR — DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18864832
  3. 🔗ODCP — DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18876594
  4. 🔗SDIA — DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18877635
  5. 🔗DEIP — DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.19004802 ← This document

5 Architecture Discussion Slots

I am offering five free 1:1 sessions for senior architects and integration experts who have read the documents and want to discuss the architecture at a structural and abstract level.

I am not excluding any professional from the APIM or CPI space — but I want to be clear: these sessions are not about how to configure APIM routing or how to get the DDCR JavaScript running. The documents cover that.

What I want to discuss is DEIP — the decision pipeline, the semantic control plane, and the structural model behind it. If that is the conversation you want to have, send me an email.

This is not a course. This is not training. The documents are already published and available for free.

If you have read the material and want to discuss architecture — send me an email: rhviana@gmail.com

What comes next

In approximately two months I will release a companion document covering how to build the complete DEIP integration map and navigate all four architectural layers in practice. I cannot commit to an exact date yet.

After that — my focus moves to IEEE. The mathematics behind DDCR and DEIP deserve a formal academic submission. That is where this work goes next.

The domain never lies.

Ricardo Luz Holanda Viana — March 2026 — Warsaw, Poland

entry.title: Re: Claude Code via MCP: Poor man's Joule, or a practical tool?
entry.content:
Day 8, a Weekend session 

First among equals

Today I wanted to test my shiny new Agentic setup that I created yesterday in the Claude ecosystem. Turns out there is this CLI command /agents, which shows you, well, the Agents that Claude Code is aware of. Who knew.

It shows my sub-Agents as correctly detected and ready:

(BTW, new /voice mode, did you see that? Sweet! Works like a charm.) 

Claude Chat proposed a few smoke tests, which I tried. E.g. this test for the codebase-explorer Agent: “Use codebase-explorer to list the contents of package $TMP”:

The screenshot also shows how the handover is done between the sub-Agent and the First among equals: By means of a temporary file. WTF! So these files will just keep accumulating there?! They'd better be reusable! (I don't think they are).

Here another test for the abap-syntax-checker, where I deliberately sent him incorrect code:

Or this test of the rap-odata-analyzer:

 

OK, but in all those tests I explicitly asked for a sub-Agent to be invoked. What if I do not ask explicitly, will it still happen? Why yes it does:

Good. Next thing would be to test the handover from sub-Agent to the parent. I’ll get to that someday, but first Skills.



About Skills

Here I have done something and I don’t very well understand what. First I’ll show the results, then I’ll explain.

Created a bunch of Skills with Claude Chat and installed them as a Project-specific /plugin:

For example, the sap-ddl-rules Skill defines forbidden names of Data Dictionary Objects fields (such as “ORDER” or “SYSTEM”, plus specifies that Currency Amounts must always have 2 decimals and Quantity Fields 3 decimals: 

  

Tested this by asking Claude to create a Database Table which would break every one these rules. He correctly invoked the Skill, flagged the discrepancies, and asked me whether I was of sound mind:

So far so good. BUT: Claude Chat told me to simply place the Skills files into the Project folder structure like this:

When I did that, Claude Code did not recognise them. I had to go through the shenanigans of installing them as a /plugin.

I think my Skill files are too long, I’ll have to revise them. Wise people say to periodically prune the Skill files to keep really only the bare minimum there. For example, right now I have this Mandatory Naming Scheme in my rap-conventions:

But I observed that the Agent already knew this by himself and used a correct naming scheme even before. Naming scheme, out!

I’d also love to Marie-Kondo the Skills into their separate lil' drawers by type (Capability Uplift vs. Encoded Preference). For, you know, easier revision and pruning. Because I'm thinking Capability Uplifts will need much more frequent revision and pruning, as the Agents uplift their own capabilities with each new Model release. Encoded Preferences should be more stable. I’m not sure if it is possible / practical to split them into files by category.

Plus, wouldn’t it be nice if individual Skills could be assigned to individual sub-Agents?

And, I need to understand the distinction between Project-level and User-level Skills - how they interact and how to organise them for the best effect.

Much more to learn about Skills, I have. Watched a YouTube video this morning where a guy designed self-improving Skills using Andrej Karpathy’s recently introduced Autoresearch loop. Dinge gibt’s, as they say in German 😯

 

So how is all this helping me in my quest to create a system which writes Fiori apps both back-end and front-end fully automatically based off of a Spec employing AI Agents, you ask? Not much. It’s helping me to perfect the back-end, which was already 90-95% correct. And that is something that even the Eclipse Wizard can do (well, you need to have the underlying Database Tables ready, but still).

However, I am learning some concepts which I shall later be able to apply to the front-end creation (I hope). And it’s fun.

 

Because the front-end creation is a mess, let me tell you. The app I vibe-coded does not work properly, and whenever I ask an Agent to fix one thing, he messes up another. This, again, is a complete shock to me. Weren't these guys supposed to be like world champions in all things JavaScript?! Seriously, I don't understand what's going on. I must be doing something disastrously wrong. 

I’ll have to concentrate on this part soon. A lot to be learned there, too. I'll let you know when I find out. Is anyone even reading this? 😅

entry.title: Component/Material availability ppds
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I Believe we have 2 ways to check component availability in ppds if we are using optimizer

option 1: use “consider upstream orders” in optimizer settings so when it schedules the order it looks at the components order receipt date/stock and schedules the order

option 2: the component ATP check at the time of conversion/release/create and it also checks the stock and receipt dates if configured in S4/ecc

so if we use option 1, is option 2 redundant? If not, in what case(s) we see a difference in result in one option over the other..

entry.title: Consolidation Plan & Actual Data
entry.content:
I want to consolidate Actual and Plan data, but I am facing an issue. The Actual data is coming from /FIGL14, which contains Cost Center, GL Account, and Amount. On the other hand, the planning Model X contains Commitment Item, GL Account, and Fund Center.

The challenge is that /FIGL14 does not contain Commitment Item and Fund Center, so I am unsure how to consolidate the data between these two structures. Could you please advise on how this can be handled?

 

If I use a left outer join, these are the values I am getting. However, the issue is that in the Actuals, the value is appearing against the GL Account instead of the Commitment Item. As a result, the actual data is showing only for the GL account, not for the commitment item.

entry.title: Re: Error with Identity Provisioning Tab in Work Zone, advanced edition
entry.content: Do as the message suggests: create a ticket for support to fix the issue. They will configure the IPS/IAS system. This is only required if you want to provision users and roles to "Applications"/"Business Content" in WZ for other systems by federation; this is different from provisioning of users to DWS. This is the same functionality provided by Standard Edition also.

entry.title: Re: How to remove IAS users from Work Zone, Advanced
entry.content: If you are using the standard transformation, you will have a group named {workzone.group.prefix}Workzone_End_User in IAS. If the user is removed from this group, the user will be removed from WZ (turns to alumini) after the provisioning job deletes the user. There could be other groups with the same prefix that are provisioned as lists or administrators. Check those too.

entry.title: Re: XSUAA not getting the subject name identifier as ID
entry.content: You need to configure SNI (Subject Name Identifier) under Single Sign-On in the Application for the subaccount in IAS.  By default, it used to be set as email. After the change, the shadow users created in the subaccount needs to be deleted so that they are created with the changed SNI as the ID.

entry.title: Re: SAP Public Cloud ADT Package deleted
entry.content:
Hello,

Thank you very much for your question.

To complete the Comments thread, I asked our internal chatbot for support. Here's the response. In case your question is still open, please check whether the response helps resolve the issue:

Steps to Retrieve Deleted or Inconsistent Files

  1. Check Git Repository History:

    • Navigate to your Git repository and check the commit history. You might find previous commits that contain the state of your package before the unintended import. You can revert to a previous commit to restore the files.

  2. Use abapGit for Export/Import:

    • If you have a backup or a previous version of your package in another system (like QAS), you can use abapGit to export the package  How to transport SAP S/4HANA Key User Custom Field… - SAP Community from there and import it back into your development system. For more information, see 

      Steps to Retrieve Deleted or Inconsistent Files

      1. Check Git Repository History:

        • Navigate to your Git repository and check the commit history. You might find previous commits that contain the state of your package before the unintended import. You can revert to a previous commit to restore the files.

      2. Use abapGit for Export/Import:

        • If you have a backup or a previous version of your package in another system (like QAS), you can use abapGit to export the package from there and import it back into your development system. For more information, see Developer Extensibility - Working with abapGit and How to transport SAP S/4HANA Key User Custom Field… - SAP Community.
        • Ensure that you have the abapGit plugin installed in your Eclipse ADT and that you have access to the QAS system.

      3. Manual Recovery:

        • If some files are still present but inconsistent, you might need to manually correct them using the ABAP Development Tools in Eclipse. This involves checking each file for errors and resolving them based on the error messages.

      4. Consult Backup Systems:

        • If your organization maintains backups of the development environment, consult with your IT department to restore the system to a previous state.

      5. Contact SAP Support:

        • If the above steps do not resolve the issue, consider reaching out to SAP support for assistance, especially if the inconsistencies are due to system errors.

      Important Considerations

      • Always ensure that you have a backup before performing major operations like imports and exports.
      • Regularly commit and push changes to your Git repository to maintain a history of changes that can be reverted if necessary.
      and 
    • Ensure that you have the abapGit plugin installed in your Eclipse ADT and that you have access to the QAS system.

  3. Manual Recovery:

    • If some files are still present but inconsistent, you might need to manually correct them using the ABAP Development Tools in Eclipse. This involves checking each file for errors and resolving them based on the error messages.

  4. Consult Backup Systems:

    • If your organization maintains backups of the development environment, consult with your IT department to restore the system to a previous state.

  5. Contact SAP Support:

    • If the above steps do not resolve the issue, consider reaching out to SAP support for assistance, especially if the inconsistencies are due to system errors.

Important Considerations

  • Always ensure that you have a backup before performing major operations like imports and exports.
  • Regularly commit and push changes to your Git repository to maintain a history of changes that can be reverted if necessary.

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Re: Good receipt and label printing for mixed purchase or...
entry.content:
Hello,

Thank you very much for your question.

To add another aspect, I asked our internal chatbot for support. Here's the response. If the question is still open, please check whether the response resolves the issue.

To address the issue of printing goods receipt labels for mixed purchase orders containing both regular production materials and free text items, you can consider the following approach:

Use of Output Parameter Determination (OPD)

  • Configure Output Types: Ensure that your Output Parameter Determination settings are configured to handle different item categories within a purchase order. This might involve setting up specific rules for label printing based on item types.

Separate Processing

  • Batch Processing: Instead of manually separating items, consider using batch processing or automation scripts that can identify and separate free text items from regular production materials. This can streamline the process and reduce manual effort.

Custom Logic

  • Implement Custom Logic: If the standard configuration does not meet your needs, consider implementing custom logic using Business Add-Ins (BAdIs) to automate the identification and processing of different item types within a purchase order.

Consult SAP Support

  • SAP Support: If these options do not resolve the issue, consulting with SAP support or your IT department for a more tailored solution might be necessary.

Unfortunately, without the ability to upload Excel files directly, you might need to explore alternative methods for sharing configuration files, such as using shared drives or cloud storage services.

For more information, see Output Parameter Determination.

Kind regards, 

Henrike

entry.title: Re: SAP S4/hana public cloud RAP to SAP build automation...
entry.content:
Hello,

Thank you very much for your question. I'm very sorry for the long delay.

I asked our internal chatbot for support. Here's the response. If the question is still open, can you please check whether the response resolves the issue?

It seems like you are encountering an issue with the workflow not being created in SAP Build Process Automation, and you are receiving a 404 error on the outbound communication. Here are a few steps and considerations that might help resolve the issue:

Check Communication Arrangement

Ensure that the communication arrangement SAP_COM_0863 is correctly set up. Create Business Partners in S/4HANA Cloud with an … - SAP Community This includes verifying that the service URL is correctly configured to point to the SAP Build Process Automation endpoint. The path /public/workflow/rest/v1/workflow-instances should be accessible, and you might need to include the environmentId as a query parameter if required by your setup.

Verify API Endpoint

The 404 error suggests that the endpoint might not be correctly specified. Double-check the API endpoint URL in your communication arrangement and ensure it matches the expected format, including any necessary query parameters like environmentId.

Check Authorization

Ensure that the communication user has the necessary roles and permissions to access the SAP Build Process Automation services. This includes roles like WorkflowInitiator and any other roles required for your specific setup.

Debugging Tips

  • Use tools like Postman to manually test the API endpoint and verify that it is accessible and returns the expected responses.
  • Check the logs in SAP BTP to see if there are any additional error messages or details that can provide more context on the 404 error.

Additional Configuration

If your setup requires specifying the environmentId, ensure that this is included in the API calls. This might need to be configured in the communication arrangement or within your RAP method logic.

By following these steps, you should be able to identify and resolve the issue with the workflow not being created.

Kind regards,

Henrike
